titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 2 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: In this letter from Wilfrid Spender to his wife, Lillian, Spender gives a detailed account of a recent battle. He also expresses anxiety that his brother Rex will soon come out to the trenches. This letter is part of a series of correspondence from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender remained in contact with the Ulster Unionist leadership, and consistently opposed Home Rule and the six-county solution.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
July2. My pr I want all our folk to
work their hardest for our men
-the thousands of brave wounded.-
Our divisions broke right through
the enemy lines in a way which
I believe no other troops have ever
done before what like our men have
done. The enemy in our front
were completely beaten. Then the
disaster happened. The divisions on
our flanks did not get up & we
were in a trap pounded on the right
by way form of shell.
I saw men going up with water
with shell all round, & deeds of
heroism with wounded.
We return to our former
position to the right but there is
no making good our men. I got no shell except some lachrymatory which gave me
a head-not yet gone-& made
my eyes smart. A few shrapnel
burst harmlessly over head, but
I would gladly have worn some
risks to have saved our men if I could. I'm afraid Rex will come
out now to add to my
anxieties. Someday we will,
please heaven, go to Begley
together.
Must end Your BW WB Spender
